dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, respond for a report of chest pain and AICD concerns.
Hello, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I'm experiencing chest pressure tonight. It's been going on for a while and I'm also having some shortness of breath and nausea.
Anna, what's your full name for us here today?
And where are we now, city-wise?
We are in Richmond, Virginia. My name is Anna Williams.
So if I asked you to squeeze my hand, you'd be okay to do that, right?
She gives a firm grasp. Fully alert and oriented with a GCS of 15-eyes 4, verbal 5, motor 6. No signs of confusion or restlessness observed.
Anna, are you able to talk or does your throat feel blocked in any way?
I feel fine. I can talk and breathe okay.
Anna, can you let me assess your breathing? Just take a normal breath for a moment.
Breaths are 16 per minute, steady and unlabored. No tachypnea noted. Any shortness of breath you're still experiencing?
A bit, but not in a severe way. I can still speak in full sentences.
Heart rate is 74, steady and regular with no pauses. Let's get the rest of the vitals now.
Blood pressure is currently 125 over 93. Skin is normal without cyanosis or diaphoresis noted.
Pulse oximetry reads 100%. That's typical even on room air. Everything seems steady at the moment.
Her pulse is strong and peripheral; no coolness or pallor in her hands or feet observed so far.
I don't see any bruising, sores, or rashes on the body. You mentioned earlier being pale-is that normal for you or a new finding?
Pale is normal for me. I've always been that way.
When did the chest pressure start, and does it change when you move or rest, or stay the same no matter what you do?
It began tonight. Feels consistent, doesn't shift no matter what I do, right in the center of my chest and no change with movement or rest.
Have you encountered similar chest symptoms before like this? Was anyone else involved or did you seek care previously for it?
Yes, about a week ago. I was taken to St. Mary's, and they found I had some coronary artery disease but nothing acute at the time.
I see you mentioned being allergic to certain medications. Can you restate your full list of allergies for documentation purposes here?
Yes-I react to penicillin, morphine, and Embeda (naltrexone). If I take any of those by accident, I can have a bad reaction.
I know you had medication history in the past, but what are you currently taking for management of your condition or otherwise right now?
I'm not on top of all the details and cannot recall exactly.
Our records show you have cardiomyopathy. Has that ever led to symptoms like this before or is today's situation particularly new for you?
Diagnosed a while back, but I've never felt anything this noticeable before. This is the most uncomfortable I've felt from it.
Could you double-check and state the date of your birth and your full legal name for our records today?
I'm Anna Williams, born in September of 1976.
How intense is the chest discomfort right now on a scale from 0 to 10 with 10 being the worst you can imagine?
Maybe a 3. Persistent, but not the worst, more uncomfortable than anything else.
What does it feel like? Sharp, heavy, or pressure?
Just pressure. It's deep, not surface-like or burning.
Has it moved to your arms, jaw, or neck-any movement of the sensation beyond the chest?
Not really. It just stays right in the middle of my body, no radiating.
Has the chest pressure gotten worse since the onset of symptoms, or has it been consistent?
It's remained consistent. No worsening.
You mentioned feeling nauseous. Have you vomited or kept anything in that you couldn't pass?
No vomiting. Just discomfort and feeling quite queasy.
So no headache or dizziness was experienced either? Just chest discomfort is the focal concern right now?
Correct. No neurological or dizziness symptoms were associated.
Any stomach or abdominal discomfort felt? Confined to just the chest symptoms?
Correct. No other discomfort beyond chest pressure.
So no abdominal pain has been reported? Just stick with chest symptoms?
That's correct. No issues in the stomach at all.
You're not leaning forward or using any position to ease your breathing, correct? Any tripoding behavior noted?
None noted during exam. She's seated normally and not in a snout or forward lean for breathing ease.
Anna, I need to set up some EKG monitoring tools for assessment of cardiac rhythm. I will first apply the 4-lead system for a basic read, then the 12-lead for a more detailed assessment, okay?
4-lead is all set. Now, I'm going to prepare the 12-lead for immediate interpretation once it's ready. That should give a more detailed overview.
The 4-lead shows a sinus rhythm. Let's interpret the 12-lead now.
The 12-lead EKG findings show nonspecific T wave abnormalities but no STE or ectopy. Have you been monitoring symptoms related to your cardiomyopathy over the past week at rest or while active?
The last follow-up, my doctor told me to monitor symptoms and not panic unless it worsened or new issues came up. I have been monitoring that.
Anna, please take this 324 mg aspirin tablet. Dissolve it under your tongue for quicker absorption. It's an emergency precaution we're doing right now.
Let me take a quick blood glucose measurement for this.
Her blood sugar is a little low but it's not concerning enough for intervention with current stable condition. We will monitor while on scene.
I will place IV access into her left arm per standing instructions in case further meds are needed.
Anna, based on her symptoms, EKG readings, and history, we are following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac-related chest pain according to field protocols.
EKG is rechecked-rhythm remains sinus with low QRS voltage in chest leads and no elevated signs at this time.
Let's take a second round of vitals-pulse is down to 69, breathing remains at 16. Still stable for transport.
Pulse ox has dropped slightly to 95%, it's acceptable and stable for now.
Blood pressure is 107 over 70 now. Small drop but remains within safe thresholds-no cause for significant intervention here.
We were told you had concerns about diversions at other hospitals. You wanted to go to MRMC, right?
Yes, I'd prefer MRMC-so let's get moving once all is ready.
Partner, let's move her onto the stretcher slowly using the stand and pivot technique. Be aware of stability and secure properly.
She is now secured-rails and straps locked. IV and EKG lines are safe, and no pulling is expected during transport.
Just double-checked-EKG is still stable, pulse 69, and pulse ox at 95%. Everything is within acceptable parameters for transport.
Just confirming vitals and stability once more. She remains within normal range. We are clear to transport-per protocol plan.
Anna has been assessed, treated, and prepared for transport to Room 29 at MRMC. Our ePCR will be with her during the handover.
IV is running and medications administered include 324 mg ASA under supervision. All protocols were followed, no deviations with full consent obtained.
She has a medical history of cardiomyopathy and a previous event a week earlier leading to a diagnosis of coronary artery disease, no intervention at that time.
Per ePCR, no STE or ectopy found. She remains stable. ASA was administered as part of standard protocol and fully documented for hospital review.
During transport, EKG has also been reviewed again and remains in sinus rhythm-no change in findings.
Just for a final check, is your pain still at a 3 on a scale of 10 and still the same deep pressure?
Yes. Soreness remains unchanged. Still a three. No elevation or decrease, same persistent pressure feel.
Still the same quality-no radiation elsewhere or any sharp or burning? That pattern is similar to your symptoms last week.
Exactly the same. Pressure is behind the sternum, persistent, and unchanging. Still only in that central spot.
Any changes at all recently? New complications or side effects?
Not at all. Just the original symptoms, no changes in anything during this time.
Confirmed arrival at MRMC with all patient vitals and supplies handed over to the hospital team for process handover.
We transferred full protocols, medication details, and EKG findings. ED staff has been informed of her condition for continued care.
All IV and EKG systems are functioning. The hospital team is prepared to continue her evaluation and management from this point.
No sign of acute hypoxia observed during assessment. All breaths were unlabored with no unusual signs of distress.
No indication of tachypnea or any signs of worsening in her breathing or respiratory effort based on earlier assessment and transport.
Based on the chief complaint and stable vitals, maintained protocol 2-1. No field deviation necessary.
Oxygen of 95% is stable-no need for additional supplementation at this time.
Respiratory rate of 16 remains. Normal and unlabored. No further action required.
We recommend immediate assessment upon arrival at the ED due to her history of cardiomyopathy and past cardiac event a week ago.
All EKG reports, medications delivered, and procedure documentation are with the patient for ED use. No further reperfusion or field diagnostics were given.
IV remains patent, and all equipment check. I will double-check the securement before finalizing transport.
All procedures are on standby if needed. IV is in place and functioning. No interventions required at this stage.
Movement positions are safe, and all systems are ready for a smooth transfer to the hospital for follow-up and further care.
IV in place and functioning. Patient in stable and safe posture for transport-no immobilization required.
No new findings noted and EKG readings remain stable. No protocol changes necessary post-reassessment.
ED is ready for patient reception. They will assume all future protocols and treatments under their own care.
I feel confident she's well-prepared for transport. All documentation and orders are in place for safe transition to next team.
Secureed in transport without issues and everything is set. Patient is ready to be handed off to MRMC ED.
